title: American cornbread
subtitle: Cast-iron skillet cornbread — crisp crust, tender crumb
excerpt: Southern-style cornbread baked in a preheated cast-iron skillet: the hot iron gives the base and sides an instant, sizzling crust while the centre stays moist and tender. Made with fine cornmeal, buttermilk, and a little fat. Served straight from the pan, cut into wedges, with plenty of butter.
sourceNotes: Cornbread is documented in American cooking from the colonial period, when maize was a primary crop and the cornmeal preparation was a staple of both Native American and settler cooking. Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544) includes several cornbread preparations. The cast-iron skillet version is the Southern tradition; the sweeter, cake-like Northern version is a distinct preparation.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely and freeze in wedges for up to 3 months. Reheat in a low oven for 10 minutes.

content.text: Southern cornbread is different from the Northern American version: it is less sweet, more savoury, and coarser in texture. The Southern version uses mainly cornmeal with a small amount of plain flour (or no flour at all), buttermilk for the liquid, and a minimum of sugar. The Northern version is closer to a sweet quick bread. This recipe is the Southern version — the one associated with chilli, ribs, and beans.

The cast-iron skillet must be preheated with fat in the oven before the batter goes in. When the cold batter hits the hot fat, it sizzles and immediately forms a crust. This is the defining characteristic of skillet cornbread — a base that is almost fried, crisp and dark at the edge, while the interior is soft and moist. Preheat for at least 5 minutes.

items.prepNote: melted, plus 1 tbsp for the skillet

content.text: Preheat the skillet and make the batter

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Place the cast-iron skillet in the oven with 1 tbsp of the {{unsalted-butter}} butter and preheat for at least 5 minutes. In a large bowl, whisk together the {{polenta}} of fine cornmeal, {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, {{baking-powder}} of baking powder, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} of bicarbonate of soda, and salt. In a jug, whisk together the {{buttermilk}} of buttermilk, {{eggs}} eggs, and remaining melted {{unsalted-butter}} of butter. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and stir until just combined.

Carefully remove the hot skillet from the oven — the butter should be sizzling. Pour the batter in immediately; it will sizzle against the hot fat. Return to the oven and bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the top is golden and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean. Rest for 5 minutes in the pan before cutting into wedges and serving.

items.cause: Skillet not hot enough when batter was poured in
items.fix: Preheat for a full 5 minutes — the butter should be sizzling and starting to brown when you add the batter; a warm skillet will not produce the same crust

items.cause: Overbaked, or too little fat in the batter
items.fix: Remove as soon as the skewer comes out clean; cornbread continues to dry as it cools in the pan

items.symptom: Top is pale even after 25 minutes
items.cause: Oven temperature too low, or oven running cool
items.fix: Check the oven with a thermometer; at 220°C fan the top should be visibly golden by 25 minutes

items.cause: Not enough fat in the preheated pan
items.fix: Use at least 1 tbsp of butter or lard to grease the skillet before preheating; a well-seasoned cast-iron pan should release cleanly

content.text: Cornbread is one of the oldest distinctly American foods, built on maize — the crop that sustained both Native American cultures and colonial settlements in North America. The Southern cast-iron skillet version is the earlier and more traditional preparation; the sweeter, cake-like Northern version emerged later and reflects different agricultural histories and taste preferences. In the South, cornbread is the bread of the table: served alongside chilli, ribs, beans, and greens, used to soak up pot liquor, and eaten for breakfast the next day with butter and sorghum. It is a practical food with a long history and very little that needs improving.
